unitTitle: Unit 38: Both, either, neither, so & nor
unitFocus: Unit 38: Both, either, neither, so & nor

sections.title: Use
items: To emphasise that two things are equally true.
To make the same statement about two people or things.

sections.title: Examples
items: **Both** Sophie and James arrived early for the meeting.
The exam was **both** long **and** difficult.
My brother and I were **both** surprised by the result.
Anna and Pete **both** enjoyed the concert.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **both** before a pronoun.
I used to work with Nina and Chris, but I've lost touch with **both of** them.

sections.title: Use
items: To present a choice between two options.

sections.title: Examples
items: You can take **either** the bus **or** the train.
He'll be **either** angry **or** relieved when he hears the news.
You can **either** apologise now **or** explain later.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **either** before a pronoun.
I like Tom and Sam — I'd be happy going to the cinema with **either of** them.

sections.title: Use
items: To indicate that neither of two options applies.

sections.title: Examples
items: She has **neither** the time **nor** the money to go on holiday.
His explanation was **neither** clear **nor** helpful.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We often use **of** with **neither** before a pronoun.
**Neither of** them remembered to bring the tickets.

sections.title: Use
items: Use **so** to add more information to a positive statement.
Use **nor** to add more information to a negative statement.

sections.title: Examples
items: Sam is very patient, and **so is** Emma.
I've never been to Tokyo, and **nor has** my sister.
He didn't understand the question, and **nor did I**.
You passed the test, and **so did** your classmate.

sections.title: Useful notes
items: We can use these structures on their own in conversation.
'I prefer tea to coffee.' — 'So do I.'
'I've never tried sushi.' — 'Nor have I.'

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with both, either or neither
exercises.title: A: Complete with both, either or neither
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is two people, so the correct answer is "Both".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "Both" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "...... my sisters love playing tennis.".

items.sentence: You can sit in ...... seat by the window.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is one of two, so the correct answer is "either".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not one nor the other, so the correct answer is "Neither".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: The film was ...... funny and exciting.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is two qualities, so the correct answer is "both".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I haven't read ...... of those books.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative + two, so the correct answer is "either".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is none of two, so the correct answer is "Neither".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Complete with so or nor + auxiliary
exercises.title: B: Complete with so or nor + auxiliary
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence so it means the same.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence so it means the same.

items.sentence: I can swim, and ...... my brother.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is agreement, so the correct answer is "so can".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so can", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "so can" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.

items.sentence: She hasn't finished yet, and ...... Tom.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative agreement, so the correct answer is "nor has".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: We've seen that film, and ...... they.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is present perfect, so the correct answer is "so have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: I don't eat meat, and ...... my cousin.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is negative, so the correct answer is "nor does".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "nor does", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Lucy was very nervous, and ...... I.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past state, so the correct answer is "so was".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "so was", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: They wouldn't change their minds, and ...... we.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is would, so the correct answer is "nor would".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "nor would", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "nor would" is a phrasal verb or fixed chunk that collocates with the rest of the sentence.
